data.title: Kratos Defense: Investing In The Future Of Autonomous Warfare
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Luke Pomichter as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The US Department of War is accelerating its shift towards a tradable autonomous system in response to near-peer threatened environments that are expected to be active within the next decade. Kratos Defense and Security Solutions (KTOS) is well-positioned for this capability set and is a significant catalyst, as its falconry production is not yet reflected in consensus estimates or forward-looking guidance. The stock currently trades at $69.83 as of the writing of this article, representing a 47.8 percent pullback from its January 2026 high of $ 134. This decline has been driven by broad market risk-off and defense-sector rotation and is not representative of company fundamentals. Current backlogs are at an all-time high, with an opportunity pipeline of approximately $13.7 billion and 8 contracts totaling approximately $629,000,000 since January. I initiate coverage of KTOS with a buy rating and a $135.00 price target, and an implied 92.9 percent upside from its current price.

This thesis is anchored in 3 business segments that are expected to expand over the next 12 to 24 months. The first is the Valkyrie drone production ramp, which is specifically excluded from management's guidance. The second is a growing hypersonics franchise with double-digit annual growth, and the third is the Prometheus solid rocket motor facility, which begins value creation in 2027.

Valkyrie Production

KTOS's financial guidance deliberately excludes Valkyrie drone production. KTOS plans to scale Valkyrie drone production from 8 to approximately 40 aircraft by 2028, contingent on government contract and budget approvals. On the most recent earnings call, CEO Eric DeMarco reported that Valkyrie had received another program-of-record designation in the US and stated that “I believe that we are in a sole source position for two additional tactical drone opportunities…” in late 2026.

The Valkyrie drone is essential to the Marine Corps force design and an initiative to equip the branch to fight in the modern era against China in the Indo-Pacific region. The constraints in the region include limited runways, large distances, and likely proximity to Chinese long-range fires. The Valkyrie fits the environment with short takeoff capability and a 3,000-nautical-mile range, designed to fly alongside F-35 Lightning II against near-peer aerial threats from China's People's Liberation Army (PLA). The Marine Corps program of record is a strong catalyst. Northrop Grumman and Kratos were awarded the MUX TACAIR contract, valued at $231.5 million for a 24-month performance period. This contract prepares the Valkyrie to fly alongside Marine Corps crewed fighters and operate in high-threat environments. As the sole prime contractor, Northrop will integrate its advanced mission kits and open-architecture autonomy software into Kratos’ XQ-58 Valkyrie.

Kratos current value is based on the initial contracted revenue, but does not capture Valkyrie production contracts, which represent incremental revenue and are expected within the next couple of years as production ramps up and the Valkyrie continues to demonstrate itself as the program of record for the Marine Corps.

Valkyrie Production Scenario Analysis

The bear case centers on program timing rather than cancellation. A 12-18-month delay in the program of record definition is a risk vector not because the Valkyrie lacks department of war support but because budget cycles may slip the acquisition timeline. If the Air Force restructured its collaborative combat aircraft program towards a single vendor production contract, then General Atomics’ incumbency and more mature platform could capture the larger program of record share and result in Kratos receiving smaller portion than the sole source position would enable. The base case assumes a program of record confirmation and a ramp of between 20 to 25 aircraft per year by 2027 with both sole source positions holding but generating revenue in line with management guidance rather than above it. A bull case it requires the full 40 per year ramp to materialize alongside allied nation export orders most likely from Australia under the UK US frameworks which would step change production volume and validate the platforms international market beyond domestic US demand. I assess the bail case to have a 20% probability, the base case to have a 50% probability, and the bull case to have a 30% probability of occurrence.

Hypersonics Franchise Rapid Growth

Management expects to approximately double Kratos hypersonic franchise revenues in 2026 to approximately $400 million, then increase by over 75% into 2027 to $700 million, contingent on program awards and integration of large solid rocket motor orders. Kratos has already received orders for 120 solid rocket motors, and the Pentagon has chosen contractors to develop highly maneuverable Mach 5-plus hypersonic missiles, including advancing in-flight steering and propulsion.

Management stated they hope to receive an additional $1 billion plus and hypersonic-related opportunities by the end of 2026, which they believe will be a sole source to create those as prime on an existing national security initiative. The Prometheus facility is the critical infrastructure underpinning the growth of the hypersonics franchise. Once operational in 2027, Prometheus would dramatically expand solid motor production capacity and address a critical bottleneck in the hypersonics industrial base.

The expected growth from 200 million in 2025 to 700 million in 2027 represents the hypersonics business alone growing approximately 15% of total revenue to 33% of the larger base revenue. Margin profiles for hypersonics are significantly better than those of legacy fixed-price drone contracts, making this growth beneficial for EBITDA expansion. Recent contract wins have demonstrated momentum for the segment. In March 2026 NSWC PHD awarded Kratos a contract worth up to $49.2 million for the production and delivery of 36 Oriole solid rocket motors and three thrust vector control production kits to support naval hypersonic programs. In February 2026, Kratos received a contract from the Joint Hypersonics Transition Office to support testing and evaluation of thermal protection systems. Separately, Rocket Lab (RKLB) was awarded a $190 million contract for 20 hypersonic test flights led by Kratos.

Hypersonics Franchise Rapid Growth Scenario Analysis

The primary risk is qualification delays for solid rocket motors, which could push the solid rocket motor ramp into 2028; however, I assess this bear case has only a 15% probability of occurring because structural demand tailwinds are strong. The base case at 50% models $600 to $700 million in hypersonics revenue for 2027, in line with management guidance, with solid rocket motor program qualifications completing in quarter three of 2026 and modest margin improvements as production scales. The bull case, which has a 35% chance of occurring, reflects asymmetric upside from supplemental contract awards tied to Indo-Pacific deterrents, which could add $50-100 million in unmodeled revenue and enable Kratos to be the solid rocket motor supplier of record for programs such as Poseidon.

The Prometheus facility is a risk to the hypersonics franchise and the most binary catalyst for the company. It will either come online in quarter one 2027, per management guidance, and generate a step-function margin improvement, or it will slip and be a significant flow to create doses for hypersonics revenue plans. The bear case, which has approximately a 20% probability, is not a deal-breaker, but it is a significant timing risk. If certification does not occur, the US government will not wait for the Prometheus facility to come online. In that case, competitors could be selected to produce solid rocket motors. The base case at 55% of occurrence has the highest base probability because facility construction is the most observable and controllable catalyst, and management has been specific about this timeline, which implies program visibility rather than aspirations. A bull case about 25% rate of occurrence would mean Prometheus coming online ahead of schedule with excess capacity. Since Kratos does not just internalize their cost savings, it would sell the solid rocket motor production to 3rd party defense primes, which would create a revenue stream that does not currently exist and would require a rerating of the business segment and subsequent revenue.

Space and Counter-UAS Expansion

Kratos’ OpenSpace platform is one of the most widely deployed software-defined satellite ground system architectures in the defense sector. The platform supports LEO, MEO, and GEO missions. It is being extended into 5G non-terrestrial network applications, following a March 2026 partnership with SKY Perfect JSAT for Japan's universal NTN initiative in the Asia-Pacific region. Commercial expansion will diversify the segment's revenue base and validate the platform's adaptability beyond defense customers. A Space Development Agency Advanced Fire Control Ground Infrastructure (AFCGI) program representing $116.7 million completed critical design review (CDR) in February 2026, with 13 months from contract award to CDR.17 The design review was completed ahead of schedule, demonstrating Kratos’ ability to execute complex programs at an accelerated pace.

In March 2026, Kratos received a $7 million production contract for a system to detect, classify, and track low-profile UAS, cruise missiles, and other aerial systems. This contract continues the company’s position as a provider of air defense hardware on a production scale. It is likely to benefit it in the long term as the proliferation of drone warfare across active conflicts accelerates customer demand.

Space and Counter-UAS Expansion Scenario Analysis

A bear case with an estimated 20% rate of occurrence is commercialization timing risk. Commercial non-terrestrial network sales cycles or longer than Department of War contracts, and if 5G non-terrestrial network adoption in the Indo-Pacific moves more slowly than partnerships imply, the segment will remain a defense-only business trading at a lower multiple. The base case at 55% assumes that open space continues when defense ground system contracts and counter UAS production contracts grow incrementally as the threat environment validates the capability. The bull case where 25% of occurrences could occur if counter-unmanned aerial systems procurement accelerates in line with drone warfare proliferation, which has been observed in Russia, Ukraine, and the Middle East over the past two years. Both an increase in counter-UAS and Kratos's transition to a dual-use infrastructure would enable multiple expansions beyond my $135 target.

Price Target

My $135 price target is derived from the scenario analysis across Kratos' business segments, along with management's guidance and estimates above consensus for programs that were excluded from guidance.

Management's guidance for revenue in 2026 is $1.4 billion, 2027 at $1.65 billion, and 2028 at approximately $1.8 billion. At 9x EV/Revenue, $1.8 billion would result in a $125 share price. My target price includes a modest premium to this base based on the weighted probability upside of individual business segments.

Within the Valkyrie segment, the bull case at 30 percent rate of occurrence would yield production of 40 aircraft per year and represent a step-function above management's current guidance. The base case which is most likely is in line with guidance at 20-25 aircraft per year, while the bear case which is least likely would stem from a production lower than 20 aircraft per year.

The hypersonics bull case includes $50-100 million in revenue reflecting supplemental contracts that accelerate due to Indo-Pacific region tensions. This estimate is in addition to the $700 million provided by management guidance for 2027. The base case aligns with management guidance of $600-700 million.

Within the Space and Counter UAS segments, the bull case reflects accelerated procurement of counter-UAS and the transition of OpenSpace to dual-use infrastructure, creating multiple expansions beyond current targets. Figure 1 shows the base and bull cases for growth into 2028.

Financial Overview

Kratos reported Q4 2025 revenue of $345.1 million, exceeding guidance by approximately $15-25 million and delivering 20 percent organic year-over-year growth. GAAP net income was $5.9 million ($0.03 per share), beating estimates by approximately 38 percent. Adjusted EBITDA was $34.1 million, and the book-to-bill ratio signaled accelerating demand across the portfolio at 1.3 times. The backlog was $1.57 billion, and the opportunity pipeline reached $13.7 billion. 2025 full-year revenue was estimated at approximately $1.17 billion, representing 18 percent organic growth. Management revenue guidance targets $1.4 billion in 2026, $1.65 billion in 2027, and approximately $1.8 billion by 2028. The $1.8 billion in revenue underpins a $125 target price at a 9x EV/Revenue multiple. A February 2026 equity offering raised approximately $1.2 billion, funding the acquisition of Orbit Technologies ($352.7 million), the Prometheus build, and program ramp capital. While this was dilutive in the near term, the funded programs should offset the dilution over a 12–24-month horizon.

Risks

While I maintain a Buy rating with a $135 target price, several risks exist that could change the thesis.

If Valkyrie Production does not increase from low-rate initial production due to supply chain issues, manufacturing delays, or government acceptance, it would warrant a significant change in the thesis and target price. Although this is the highest conviction segment of the thesis, failure in this segment would reduce the price target to $95-105, and I would reassess the rating from Buy to Hold.

The bull case assumes the Prometheus facility is online in Q1 2027 as planned. Any movement to the right in that schedule defers revenue and would compress near-term EBITDA margins.

KTOS's hypersonics franchise depends on completing system qualification milestones, which could include failures or anomalies that could delay program-of-record designations. Delays or significant failures would extend the pre-revenue phase and reduce the segment's overall contribution to the bull case.

Forward-looking Position

KTOS provides a play into an attritable defense system at an affordable price at a moment when militaries are accelerating their transition to this doctrine. The approximately $69.00 price represents a discount from January 2026 highs and approximately 92% upside to my price target of $135. The pullback is macro-driven, and Kratos has strengthened since January with multiple awards and an equity offering that provides capital for future growth. The market has not yet produced in vulgar reproduction the Prometheus capacity or the full scope of the hypersonics franchise. The program-of-record designation represents a step-function in revenue that consensus estimates do not currently reflect. As these catalysts materialize over the next 12 to 24 months, the gap between the current price and intrinsic value should close.

data.title: Up To 9% Yields With Strong Growth Potential To Buy On The Drop
data.description:
The recent pullback in several energy midstream (AMLP) names has created an attractive opportunity to buy high-quality, high-yielding dividend growth names at an attractive valuation. Today's article will discuss two opportunities that yield up to 9.1% today.

A Diversified Midstream Giant

The first opportunity we're going to discuss is ONEOK, Inc. (OKE), which is one of the larger and better diversified energy infrastructure companies (MLPX) out there, with about 60,000 miles of pipelines spanning:

  • NGL gathering.

  • Fractionation.

  • Natural gas gathering.

  • Processing.

  • Transportation.

  • Storage.

  • Refined products transportation and storage.

  • Crude oil gathering and transportation.

OKE is structured as a C corporation, so it issues a 1099 tax form, which makes it popular with many investors who wish to avoid the hassles of the K-1 tax form. Additionally, about 90% of its earnings come from fees, which gives it a very stable cash flow profile.

AI-Linked Tailwinds and Proven Resilience

OKE is engaged with over 40 counterparties for data center and power generation projects, which gives it attractive AI-linked growth potential. Additionally, it has already achieved about $625 million in cumulative synergies from its Magellan and Lincoln Medallion acquisitions and expects to continue achieving more, with about $75 million in additional synergies expected by the end of this year.

It has also grown its adjusted EBITDA for 12 straight years, despite facing a plethora of commodity price volatility cycles during that period, including COVID-19, thereby demonstrating its significant cash flow generation resilience. In addition, OKE is well diversified by product and geography, which makes it even more stable from a cash flow and long-term growth potential perspective.

A High-Yield Permian Play

The other opportunity we discussed today is Delek Logistics Partners, LP (DKL), which is a K-1-issuing MLP that is focused on the Permian Basin across crude gathering, natural gas gathering and processing, water gathering, disposal, storage, and terminaling services. Thus, DKL is much smaller and more concentrated than OKE, which makes it a higher risk; however, investors are compensated for this through its attractive yield and growth profile. As it recently posted record adjusted EBITDA for 2025, DKL has numerous growth projects that are just coming online that should continue driving growth moving forward. Its strong growth is also evidenced by its 13 consecutive years of distribution growth, including 52 straight quarters of distribution increases.

Balance Sheet and Leverage: A Key Risk to Watch

When it comes to their balance sheets, OKE is investment grade and is working to get leverage down to its long-term 3.5x target, which is well in line with investment-grade metrics and below some of its peers, like Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI). As part of this effort, OKE paid down a whopping $3.1 billion of long-term debt in 2025 by buying back debt on the open market at a discount, thanks to its significant retained distributable cash flow.

DKL has its leverage at 4.4x adjusted EBITDA, which is on the higher end of the spectrum in the midstream sector. That said, DKL has almost $1 billion worth of available liquidity under its credit facilities, which gives it sufficient flexibility, given that its total net debt stands at $2.37 billion. Though it still has ongoing growth capex for several projects that will likely continue to push leverage higher this year, as growth projects continue coming online and its coverage ratio improves from its current fairly stretched 1.2x level on a distributable cash flow basis, DKL should begin to deleverage more aggressively. However, there is a risk in the meantime that its leverage, combined with the fairly narrow coverage ratio of its distribution, could force it to cut if either some of its growth projects hit a hiccup or the economy gets into a rough patch.

The Lower-Risk Option

Given these factors, it is clear that OKE is much lower risk than DKL, as it generates $8.1 billion in adjusted EBITDA that is spread across multiple geographies, whereas DKL's total EBITDA is just around $540 million and concentrated entirely in the Permian Basin. Additionally, DKL is heavily reliant on one counterparty, namely Delek US Holdings, Inc. (DK), whereas OKE is diversified across multiple counterparties.

Moreover, OKE's dividend is very well covered, currently to the tune of over 2.1 times distributable cash flow based on projections for 2026, whereas DKL's coverage ratio is just around 1.2 times. This means OKE has little risk of a dividend cut and should be able to continue growing its dividend at a steady pace while investing aggressively for growth and continuing to deleverage. DKL, on the other hand, will be stretched in all directions and, if things do not continue to go well, could have to give up its distribution growth and potentially even cut it in a worst-case scenario.

The Upside Case: Growth Profiles and Yield Comparison

That said, if everything goes well for DKL, it should be able to generate significant total return outperformance. While OKE has an expected normalized earnings per share CAGR through 2030 of about 7.6%, DKL has an even higher 10.9% distributable cash flow per unit CAGR projected through 2030 based on analysts' consensus estimates.

Additionally, its distribution yield is about 400 basis points higher at a 9.1% level compared to OKE's 5.1% level. Thus, while both have strong dividend growth track records, OKE is a lower-risk, lower-yielding, lower-total-return-profile investment, whereas DKL is a higher-yielding, higher-risk, higher-total-return-profile investment.

Who Should Buy Each, And Why Both Are Worth Owning Now

While both will be subject to commodity price volatility in terms of their underlying cash flows and their equity prices, for more risk-averse investors, OKE is a much better pick. For those who are seeking to maximize income and total returns and are willing to take on extra risk to do so, DKL is the better option. In particular, DKL is appealing if you already have large, diversified holdings in your portfolio, like an Energy Transfer LP (ET) or an Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), or even KMI. It's also worth noting that OKE is a 1099 issuer while DKL is a K-1 issuer, so that may make a decisive difference for many investors as well.

Ultimately, I like both right now and think that either one is a buy on the dip. While I do not hold either in my portfolio at the moment, they are exactly the type of opportunities I could see myself buying back into after having sold both at large gains in the recent past. I think that they both fit the profile of the type of investments I look for to build my 7–8% yielding, total return outperforming, and below-market-beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: USD/CHF Price Analysis: Bulls Eye Key Resistance After Base Formation
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

USD/CHF Daily Chart: Building a Base Above Key Support

The daily time frame shows USD/CHF (USD:CHF) in a recovery phase following the sharp sell-off witnessed in early 2026. After bottoming out near the 0.7600 handle, the pair has formed a series of higher lows, currently supported by an ascending trendline.

Price action is currently sandwiched between the 50-day MA (0.7845) and the 100-day MA (0.7865). A daily candle close above the 100-day MA would be a significant bullish signal, suggesting a shift in medium-term momentum.

However, the overhead 200-day MA at 0.7937 remains the "line in the sand" for bulls. Until that level is reclaimed, the overall daily structure remains cautious.

The RSI is hovering around the 50 midline, indicating a lack of clear directional conviction at this stage.

H4 Chart: Testing the Golden Cross Zone

Moving down to the 4-hour chart, we see a more defined bullish structure. USD/CHF has successfully pushed above the 0.7828 horizontal support level, which previously acted as a ceiling during the consolidation in mid-April.

Notably, the H4 chart shows the 100-period MA crossing above the 200-period MA, often a precursor to sustained bullish momentum.

Price is currently testing the 200-period MA (0.7887). A sustained break above this level would open the door for a retest of the psychological 0.8000 resistance area. The RSI on this time frame is rising toward 65.00, suggesting there is still room for further upside before reaching overbought conditions.

H1 Chart: Intra-day Scenarios and Key Levels

The 1-hour chart provides a granular view of the current breakout attempt. Price has found a foothold above all three major moving averages (50, 100, and 200), which are now beginning to fan out, supporting the bullish thesis.

data.title: The New Void Within Apple
data.description:
Ten years ago, I posted an article on SA titled The Void Within Apple, in which I pointed out that the death of Steve Jobs had left a vacancy at Apple Inc. (AAPL), the Product Architect, which hadn’t been filled. Arguably, it never really has been, as demonstrated by various product misfires over the years, such as the cylindrical Mac Pro and the Apple Vision Pro. Apple has mainly succeeded in carrying forward Jobs’s vision, as in Apple Silicon. But AI arrived too late to enter into Jobs’s long-range thinking, and now a new void has emerged at Apple: the AI Architect.

Apple's product struggles after Jobs

First, let me recap what I mean by a Product Architect, as I wrote ten years ago:

Product architects are generalists, not specialists. They may have a technical or engineering background, but they also have a wide range of interests beyond that. The key role of the product architect is to figure out how to turn innovative technologies into successful products.

First and foremost, there has to be an understanding that innovative technology doesn't guarantee a successful product. Products that feature innovation for its own sake often fail in the marketplace, because they don't address a need of the consumer.

On the other hand, product architecting is not simply a matter of doing a market survey, asking consumers what they need, and then giving it to them. Invariably, such surveys fail to identify unmet needs or opportunities for new technologies to create needs where none existed.

The ability of Steve Jobs to see the product nascent in a lab experiment was his true genius, but he also had the ability to guide product development efforts (such as the development of the Mac, iPod, iPhone, etc.), making crucial technology decisions along the way.

I think Jony Ive was intended to assume that role, and he may even have thought he was doing that. But when I look back on the products developed during his tenure at Apple, I see his aesthetic sense overruling common sense.

For instance, at the time it was introduced in 2013, the cylindrical Mac Pro was hailed for its innovation and attractiveness. But it was a disaster in the marketplace. The design was inflexible, and its capabilities, especially in graphics, were easily surpassed by competitors for far less money.

The MacBook Pros were stripped of useful interfaces such as Ethernet, USB, SD slots, and MagSafe in favor of all Thunderbolt ports. This was apparently aesthetically pleasing to Ive, but Apple eventually walked back most of the changes, to the relief of MacBook Pro users.

After Ive’s departure in 2019, Apple’s products seem to have become design-by-committee exercises. The most egregious example is the Apple Vision Pro.

It’s as if they couldn’t decide what they wanted it to do, so they decided to just make it do everything. As is usually the case with Apple products, it’s a technological tour de force and often a wonderful experience.

But it’s also bulky, heavy, and impractical to wear for long periods of time, although some reviewers actually tried. And because of its enormous complexity and sophistication, it’s way too expensive. It’s another example of a highly innovative Apple product that has failed miserably in the marketplace.

I don’t fault outgoing CEO Tim Cook for these missteps. Jobs always understood that Cook “isn’t a product guy.” And Cook has done a magnificent job of leading the company to tremendous growth. He also made exactly the right call when he committed the company to Apple Silicon and brought Apple Silicon to the Mac.

But the lack of a product architect still stands as a weakness that design by committee can only compensate for to some extent. Now, maybe new CEO John Ternus can assume the role of product architect, but I doubt it.

He’s probably too much of an engineer. Engineers tend to like technical complexity for its own sake. I know, I suffer from that as well. But the engineer’s ideal product often fails in the marketplace. Engineers are too focused to be good generalists.

The lack of an AI architect jeopardizes what Siri was meant to be

John Giannandrea has been the senior vice president for Machine Learning and AI Strategy since 2018. We might call him the AI Architect. When Apple Intelligence fell flat and the new, more intelligent Siri was delayed, Giannandrea was effectively demoted in early 2025.

Last week, Giannandrea officially left Apple, and his job functions reportedly have been split between software chief Craig Federighi, services head Eddy Cue, and operating chief Sabih Khan. There’s that design-by-committee approach that works so well.

The constant Siri delays and changing goals and approaches for Siri suggest that Apple has simply lost its way. According to Bloomberg’s Mark Gurman, Apple is pivoting (once again) its AI strategy towards “an App Store approach”:

...Apple is pursuing a two-pronged strategy: embedding just enough AI into its operating systems to keep users from defecting to Android, while opening Siri and Apple Intelligence to third-party services. This approach leverages Apple’s hardware, makes its products more customizable, and keeps the company in control of its ecosystem.

This sounds like a “we don’t know what to do, so let’s let third parties do everything” approach. Apple has lost its way. Apple needs an AI architect to provide vision, leadership, and direction.

Apple management should read an article by fellow SA contributor Dilantha De Silva, Apple: Perplexity Computer Ships What Siri Could Not. Perplexity Computer is a new service from Perplexity AI. De Silva writes:

Perplexity Computer is the agentic AI orchestration platform that was launched in late February. This is not a Gen AI platform that will answer your questions. Rather, Perplexity Computer can help you execute your workflows. In other words, it can get work done for you. For example, Perplexity Computer can run a workflow command for you even when you are not sitting in front of your computer, actively talking to Perplexity. Some of the tasks Perplexity Computer can do for you include conducting deep research, deploying applications, data entry, and even continuously tracking competitors. When you start using Perplexity Computer, you'd feel as if a dozen AI employees are working for you simultaneously.

On April 16, the Perplexity Computer Mac app was launched. I recommend that you watch the launch video here. What this native Mac app does is securely connect to local files seamlessly, giving Perplexity Computer the power it needs to work with local files and Mac native apps (such as iMessage and Apple Mail) to complete tasks autonomously/semi-autonomously, depending on user preferences.

De Silva then goes on to complain at length that Apple hasn’t been able to do with Siri what Perplexity has been able to do. I don’t agree with all his conclusions, but what I resonated with in the article was that he understood what the “more intelligent Siri” should be: an AI agent that works on behalf of the user.

De Silva gets that AI is merging with computer operating systems, becoming the computer user interface. Now it appears that Apple is turning away from this, if Gurman’s reporting is correct. This is completely wrong.

Why has the new Siri proved so difficult

Who better than Apple, which has always touted its hardware/software integration, to create the ultimate AI agent for its devices? At first, this was clearly Apple’s direction for Siri.

Siri would be able to access your files and personal data, which it can’t do now, and perform actions on your device. Why has this proved so difficult? It all comes down to security and privacy.

Perplexity Computer is hosted in the cloud. When it accesses your personal data or files or takes action on your device, it’s doing so over the internet. So your personal data has to be stored in the cloud, where it can be hacked and stolen.

All the big AIs that Apple is supposedly so inferior to are hosted in the cloud, where there are unlimited compute resources and no compunctions about accessing and using user data.

That’s what makes these AIs like Gemini, ChatGPT, and Perplexity Computer so effective. Everyone in the business knows this is a huge security problem. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) even introduced its version of another agentic AI app, OpenClaw, called NemoClaw, to try to address the security concerns. OpenClaw runs locally, but it can have unfettered access to the internet.

Apple’s solution to the security problem has been to use a combination of on-device AI and what Apple calls Private Cloud. The new Siri would be hosted in Private Cloud, but no user data would be permanently stored after a given Siri session.

This presents problems because part of what makes the cloud AIs so effective is that they learn from their interactions and observations of user behavior. That’s where the “magical” ability to anticipate the user comes from.

Siri can’t learn from its user interactions the way the other cloud AIs can. Any long-term data storage has to be on the user’s device, which has limited capacity. And the data has to be uploaded to Siri when the user makes a request to Siri, and Siri has to spend a few milliseconds relearning what it can about the user and past interactions.

That’s not the least of Apple’s problems with Siri. Private Cloud runs on Apple Silicon, Apple-designed and manufactured servers. This is also to provide greater privacy and security, but there were delays in setting up the server factory, which wasn’t completed until last year. Then Apple has had to build up enough inventory to stand up a large data center to handle all the Private Cloud requests.

Investor takeaways: Apple’s on-device AI future

Many people don’t understand how dangerous agentic AI hosted in the cloud is. As powerful as Perplexity Computer is, I wouldn’t dare use it.

I hope Apple hasn’t given up on creating an agentic AI, the new Siri, that protects user privacy, because it’s desperately needed. But it may be proving too difficult in the near term. And this is where the AI architect is really essential. The AI architect needs to preserve that vision of an agentic AI that preserves user privacy and security while being enabled by Apple’s device knowledge.

Maybe Apple will need to compromise on user privacy in the near term, at least to the extent of preserving user data in Private Cloud for future use. I would trust Apple more than I would trust Perplexity, Google, or Microsoft.

Apple needs to stay the course in building intelligent operating systems for its devices. The merging of AI and computer operating systems is inevitable. Apple doesn’t need to build a general-purpose chatbot to compete with the likes of OpenAI. Apple does need to make sure it isn’t left behind by the next generation of intelligent operating systems.

And this is where Apple has a unique advantage. Apple Silicon is great for running local AI models and agents. And Apple Silicon is far more efficient than any other computing architecture, whether CPU- or GPU-based.

Eventually, the problem of secure agentic AI operating systems gets solved by moving the AI completely on-device. You can already do that on Apple’s more powerful Macs, such as the Mac Studio. On my 512 GB Mac Studio, I run very large AI coding assistants integrated directly into Xcode, and the results have been truly impressive.

And in time, this will be true of mobile devices as well. Then, the operating system and the AI will be truly merged. Until then, I remain long Apple and rate it a Buy.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Intel Chips Fly High
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Intel (INTC) surges as Q1 results, guidance blow past Wall Street's forecast.(00:14) U.S. soldier charged after allegedly earning $400,000 betting on Maduro’s removal. (01:53) Nike (NKE) cuts 1,400 jobs in another layoff round this year. (03:20)

This is an abridged transcript.

Intel (INTC) is up 21% in premarket action.

The semiconductor company reported first-quarter results and guidance that were well above Wall Street's forecast.

For the period ending March 28, Intel said it earned an adjusted $0.29 per share as revenue rose 7.2% year-over-year to come in at $13.58B.

A consensus of analysts expected Intel to earn an adjusted $0.01 per share on $12.36B in revenue during the quarter.

“The next wave of AI will bring intelligence closer to the end user, moving from foundational models to inference to agentic. This shift is significantly increasing the need for Intel’s CPUs and wafer and advanced packaging offerings,” said Lip-Bu Tan, Intel CEO.

Seeking Alpha analyst Louis Gerard said the results “somewhat delivered” as the company has become “the biggest show me story on the market.”

“The company is basically cleaning its balance sheet early which is not reflected in the non-GAAP metrics,” Gerard said via email. “The management is effectively clearing decks, taking the pain now to potentially show a cleaner growth in 2027.”

Looking ahead, Intel expects second-quarter revenue to be between $13.8 and $14.8B, with the midpoint of $14.3B well above the $13.04B analysts were anticipating. It also anticipates earning an adjusted $0.20 per share, compared to the estimates of $0.09 per share.

A U.S. Army soldier allegedly earned over $400,000 on Polymarket (POLYMARKET) by trading with classified information about the timing of a U.S. military operation to capture Nicolás Maduro in Venezuela.

The Justice Department announced Thursday an indictment charging Gannon Ken Van Dyke with "unlawful use of confidential government information for personal gain, theft of nonpublic government information, commodities fraud, wire fraud, and making an unlawful monetary transaction."

As alleged in the indictment, Van Dyke participated in the planning and execution of the U.S. military operation to capture Maduro, and used his access to classified information about that operation to personally profit.

Van Dyke bet a total of about $33,034 on certain outcomes while in possession of classified nonpublic information about the operation.

Prediction market platform Kalshi (KALSHI) meanwhile said on Wednesday that it has suspended and penalized three congressional candidates - from Minnesota, Texas, and Virginia - over alleged “political insider trading” tied to their campaigns.

Nike (NKE) on Thursday announced a second round of layoffs this year, impacting nearly 1,400 roles.

The layoffs will be in the Global Operations team, with the majority concentrated in its technology department, the company said.

This will affect employees across North America, Asia, and Europe and represent less than 2% of the company’s total global headcount, according to CNBC.

Earlier this year, Nike cut 775 jobs at the company's distribution centers.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

TSMC hits record high after Taiwan loosens fund investment limits; Taiex climbs 3.3%

Spirit Airlines bailout? Trump says 'I think we'd just buy it'

Microsoft offers buyouts to 7% of U.S. workforce amid AI shift: report

Stock index futures point to a mixed open as volatility picked up and hopes of a near-term resolution between the U.S. and Iran faded.

President Trump said in a social media post that he ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill any boat” caught laying mines in the strait. He also said the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by three weeks.

Crude oil is up 1.5% at $97. Bitcoin is down 0.8% at $77,000. Gold is flat at $4,695.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.8% and the DAX is down 0.6%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Coursera (COUR) -10% - Shares fell sharply after a wider-than-expected GAAP loss and declining free cash flow overshadowed otherwise solid operating metrics, including double-digit consumer revenue growth and a record 7.6M new learners in Q1.

Economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Consumer Sentiment
